text:
$INTC $ASX $AMKR

Advanced Packaging Continues its Expansion

3D Glass Solutions (3DGS), a U.S.-based company backed by Intel Capital ($8 million invested across rounds) and Lockheed Martin, has broken ground on India’s first advanced 3D chip packaging plant

Technology Focus: Glass interposers and substrates for advanced 3D packaging, heterogeneous integration, and vertically integrated operations (substrate manufacturing + assembly + packaging). Glass substrates offer advantages in thermal performance, signal integrity, and high-density interconnects compared to traditional organic substrates.

Target Market:
-High-performance computing (HPC)
-Artificial Intelligence (AI)
-Defense
-RF / 5G
-Automotive
-Photonics and co-packaged optics (CPO)

his is one of the most advanced packaging projects approved under India’s semiconductor initiative (described by officials as “truly advanced” and novel technology).

It marks Intel’s continued push to expand its global advanced packaging footprint.

Google and Amazon are in active discussions with Intel for advanced packaging services for their custom ASICs with potential adoption of Intel's EMIB or next generation EMIB T technology for Google's TPUs and Amazon's Trainium chips. Customer commitments for these packaging deals are expected in the second half of 2026 with possible updates at Intel's earnings call on April 23. Intel CFO David Zinsner noted that customers are ready to commit and even prepay with potential deals reaching billions of dollars describing advanced packaging as now the more interesting and higher margin part of the foundry business with gross margins around forty percent. The interest stems largely from TSMC's constrained advanced packaging capacity which remains heavily concentrated in Taiwan creating supply security concerns for hyperscalers. Intel is actively expanding its packaging capacity including a new Malaysia complex set to become operational in 2026 for assembly and testing and its New Mexico fabs already in mass production of advanced 3D packaging since early 2024. EMIB T the upgraded version of Intel's embedded multi die interconnect bridge technology adds through silicon vias for better power delivery and signal integrity and is designed to support very large AI packages. TSMC is responding by building its own advanced packaging facility in the United States with construction starting in the second quarter of 2026 and operations targeted for late 2027 to 2028. This development highlights growing momentum in Intel's foundry turnaround as it leverages packaging strengths to attract major AI customers amid tight industry capacity."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2042094356946153708"}}, "rest_id": "2042096226355818503", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "135571", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2046738113142345773", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "16428", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Asian supply chain leaks just revealed the Holy Grail of wearables, and the market is completely missing the underlying silicon photonics chokepoint. 🚨 

Let’s connect the dots on Point 6 regarding the Apple Watch Ultra and the non-invasive blood glucose sensor. If you want to know who is building the light engine, look at the attached slide from $SIVE / $SIVEF. 🧵👇

1️⃣ The Leak: Miniaturized Lasers for Health
According to the supply chain leak below, Apple is targeting April next year for its blood glucose feature. The tech? Non-invasive spectroscopy using miniaturized lasers to collect reflected wavelengths through the skin.

2️⃣ The F100 Client Connection
Look at the attached slide from Sivers Semiconductors ($SIVE). A mystery "US Fortune 100" client has invested ~$18M in NRE to develop custom Indium Phosphide (InP) optical sensors for "Consumer devices" (notice the smartwatch icon). Apple is Top 5 on the Fortune 100.

3️⃣ The Timeline Matches Perfectly
Sivers delivered an order of 50,000 chips in Q1 2024 to this F100 client. 50k is not mass production—it is the exact volume required for EVT/DVT (Engineering & Design Validation Testing) prototype runs. A prototype run in early 2024 aligns perfectly with an aggressive commercial launch in Spring 2027.

4️⃣ The True TAM (Total Addressable Market)
The slide explicitly states that when this project enters the volume phase, the Request For Proposal (RFP) ranges from $30M to several $100M per year.

While retail investors are looking at historical P/E ratios of a legacy telecom business, they are completely blind to the fact that $SIVE has spent years building the miniaturized InP laser engine for the biggest consumer health revolution of the decade.
The data is right there for anyone willing to do the math. 1+1=2. 🍏⌚️

⚠️ Disclaimer: Supply chain leaks are unverified rumors by nature and timelines can shift. However, investing is about connecting high-probability data points. The technological and chronological overlap here is simply too precise to ignore. As always, DYOR.

text:
I’m now long $POET

$POET is one of the wildest turnaround stories in photonics, not so long ago I was bearish with the hard dilution around this company. 

Rewind to early 2024. Company was near insolvent. Nearly ran out of cash. Forced into an emergency raise. Share count has nearly 4x’d in two years.
Fast forward to today:
+$ 313M cash on the balance sheet (vs ~$1.3B market cap)
+ Just raised $150M in January at $7.25 from institutional investors
+ 152M shares out vs 40M-ish pre-2024
+ Marvell orders now confirmed by the CFO
+ Foxconn and Luxshare partnerships already disclosed
+ Strategic tie-up with $SIVE on integrated light sources

The dilution was brutal. But look at what it bought: a wafer-level optical interposer platform that Marvell, Foxconn, Luxshare, and Sivers are all plugged into.

The old POET was a story stock with $41K in revenue. The new POET just 25x’d revenue YoY and is embedded in the CPO supply chain right next to $SIVE

A raising phoenix as it seems.

I’m long since yesterday. Great continuation today up 20% and still a long way to re rate.

Ooo the irony...You point this out and you immediately get two Swedish jante guys chasing you...

Since the spending time on the wrong things and don't see the photonics ecosystem revaluation..they don't get it..

This picture show a "peer" and customer to Sivers..Just look at the valuation..Sivers looks cheep in this comparison. What the don't get is this revaluation is about something else, not at all about SIVE in it self..it is about the ecosystem below AI, hyperscalers, Nvidia/ AMD and so on...I told them all many years
 ago that this will happen. But the same thing then but but EBIT is bad...EBIT is bad still due to write off capitalized R&D and M&A...mostly none CF items. 
God speed! 🍺

text:
**3 of them.**

$SHMD (SCHMID Group) supplies wet-process equipment, plating, and automation for:

- High-end / advanced PCBs (including mSAP lines for AI server boards)
- ABF substrates & IC-substrate manufacturing
- Advanced packaging (panel-level packaging systems for AI/HPC chiplets)

They don’t touch lasers, silicon photonics, power delivery, memory, hybrid bonding, thermal/liquid cooling, or the grid/power side. Their core focus is the PCB + substrate + panel packaging layer that’s currently a real AI supply-chain pinch point.

Proof-of-concept prototypes → hybrid systems → software stack → wafer-scale production.

AI inference/training at 10-1000x speed with tiny power draw. Data centers breathe easier. Edge AI explodes. 

Photonics could be the final hardware frontier—faster & greener than GPUs. Quantum's cooler cousin just showed up. Game changer for AI timelines.

Shkreli just positioned himself as the gateway to Artificial General Intelligence and Artificial Super Intelligence. 🚀

text:
A lot of people are saying “invest in photonics, quantum, deep tech”

Reality is most retail including me don’t actually have an edge there

Unless you’re building this stuff or working in it every day, you’re mostly guessing

Like I can look at $COHR $LITE $POET and read about them

But do I really know what separates one from the other at a deep level?

And more importantly would I know when one quietly falls behind? Probably not

Same thing with chips

I see $NVDA $AMD $ASML $TSM all winning from the data center buildout
But if something shifts and one starts losing would I catch it early? Unlikely

That’s why I own $SMH than pretend I can out analyze engineers

Your edge isn’t chasing the most technical thing

Your edge is understanding what you actually use and see every day

I use $META daily. Everyone around me uses it. I can feel if engagement is going up or down

That’s real signal

There’s always going to be a “next bottleneck” or the next shiny theme

Most people chasing it are just rotating narratives not actually investing with an edge

Stay within what you understand my friends

Unlikely That’s why I own $SMH than pretend I can out analyze engineers Your edge isn’t chasing the most technical thing Your edge is understanding what you actually use and see every day I use $META daily. Everyone around me uses it. text:
@bdinvestingg The “photonics kills copper” take misses the point.
Photonics moves data. Copper delivers power.
With ~200GW of data centres in backlog, that’s 5–8M tonnes of copper demand waiting.
AI isn’t reducing copper—it’s increasing it.

➡️Clouds held and great higher low price action into 10am so I scaled in more as PA got tight
➡️Got the move to HOD > 60 breakout!
➡️Locked into strength over 60
➡️wanted 65 on this but gave up midday
➡️Stopped out when 5/12 clouds broke

text:
Copper is dead. AI data centers need light — and photonics companies can’t build fast enough to keep up.

$LITE – Makes the lasers inside every optical transceiver. No LITE, no light.
$AAOI – Builds transceivers that convert electrical signals to optical. The translator.
$MRVL – Designs the silicon brains that control photonic data flow at speed.
$GLW – Invented optical fiber. Still makes the glass the internet runs through.

text:
Three distinct approaches to quantum hardware currently dominate the sector's market activity: cloud-accessed trapped ions, quantum annealing with hybrid solvers, and room-temperature integrated photonics.

The industry is not converging on a single architecture. It is branching out, with different players betting on fundamentally unique paths to commercial value. This diversity proves the field is in its experimental prime, where the ultimate hardware winner remains undecided and may never narrow to just one modality.

Yet the true common thread is accessibility. Through cloud delivery, advanced developer frameworks, and intuitive circuit creation platforms, the race has permanently evolved. It is no longer just a raw contest of qubit counts. The defining metric is how quickly platforms can put usable quantum capability into the hands of enterprise teams.

The commercial layer of quantum computing is forming in real time. The organizations building robust bridges between complex physics and practical business problems are the ones shaping what comes next #QuantumComputing

text:
什么是硅光子（Silicon Photonics）
最简单的理解：
传统芯片用电子传输信息，硅光子用**光子（光）**传输信息。

传统方式：电信号 → 铜线传输 → 慢、热、耗能
硅光子：光信号 → 光纤传输 → 快、冷、省电

硅光子在AI里的具体应用
Co-Packaged Optics（CPO，共封装光学）：
这是最重要的趋势，把光模块直接封装到交换机芯片旁边：

传统方式：
交换机芯片 → 电信号 → 插拔式光模块 → 光纤

CPO方式：
交换机芯片 + 硅光子模块（合为一体）→ 光纤

text:
as promised, here is more on photonics 

1 with buy signals, 7 with sell signals

Sector running hot. Signals have to scream Sell due to overextended. But a 'Sell' signal doesn't always mean a crash; sometimes it just means a rest stop. It’s possible the engines are just cooling down before the next leg up. The trend isn't broken … til proven otherwise

$MRVL , $AAOI , $AEHR , $LPTH, $LWLG , $UMC , $NOK , 
$SIVEF

HOW TO DECODE MY SYSTEM (updated 4/23/26):

System is layered with 4 buy and sell signals using different methods to ensure no move is missed, and also to show the confidence of the correct move.

HI CERTAINTY SELL:
any shape on top of candle (♦️, 🟪, ◼️, regardless of color)=

HI CERTAINLY BUY:

text:
Which earnings are you looking forward to most this week?

We’re getting major updates across several key trends:
1. Photonics/Networks: $GLW, $CLS, $APH
2. Memory: $STX, $SNDK, $WDC
3. Grid/Energy: $BE, $XOM, $CVX
4. Finance: $HOOD, $SOFI
5. Magnificent 7: $META, $AMZN, $GOOGL, $MSFT, $AAPL
6. AI Software: $ZETA, $LMND

Very exciting week ahead. 

If Trump doesn’t do anything out of the box, it looks like we’re in for a really strong week for the S&P 500.

I’ll be paying especially close attention to sentiment in the statements, execution, forward guidance, and industry insights - particularly in Memory, Photonics, and Energy. 

My top focus: $SNDK, $GLW, and $BE.

Some analysts are calling for 40% revenue growth at $ZETA - really curious to see if they deliver.

On $HOOD and $SOFI, I’m not expecting extraordinary results. Robinhood in particular remains heavily tied to crypto market volatility and success which has recently been low.

As for the Mag 7, five of them report this week - crazy!

I’ll be watching cloud revenue growth closely at $AMZN, $GOOG, and $MSFT. 

For $META, I want updates on AI applications across their business segments, AI project progress, and ad spend trends. 

For $AAPL, I’m looking for order numbers on their recent product launches plus any fresh details on AI projects and integrations.

text:
Amkor $AMKR is currently playing out a similar pattern to many other photonics/optics/AI supply chain companies; the Bull Flag breakout!

After months of consolidation we’ve seen a huge breakout over the last few weeks, but where do we go from here?

$AMKR is currently up about 98% YTD and is looking stretched now on almost all timeframes.

Short term; $AMKR likely needs to consolidate for a few weeks, to cool down, allow the moving averages to catch up, for institutions to accumulate more shares and to build momentum for another move up.

Fundamentally; $AMKR is the go-to advanced packaging supplier for both $AVGO and $TSMC - if Amkor can continue scaling production, expect this to continue to feed through into revenues (and profits) and further long term growth.

If $AMKR can continue to execute, their stock will continue to climb long term.

text:
I watched sunlight flicker on water.

The light appeared. Disappeared. Appeared again.

I thought: what if that IS a logic gate?

That question became 28 verified claims,
a complete compiler, and a new computing paradigm.

🧵 The Photonic Logic Chip. Thread below. 👇

#photonics

text:
$POET v $MRVL

David Lazovsky is a mechanical engineer who has over 50 patents in semiconductors and photonics. He became Executive Chairman of $POET in 2017. Before his arrival, $POET focused on GaAs. It was Lazovsky who pushed $POET to develop their optical interposer. He left $POET in 2020 and created Celestial AI because he was more keen on developing the whole photonics ecosystem. The essence is how to move data around multiple logical chips and memory, optically. Optical Multi-Die Interconnect Bridge, US Patent #12399333 and Photonic Memory Fabrics, US Patent #12271595. His development at Celestial AI was based on the optical interposer from $POET. Celestial AI signed a purchase agreement with $POET. 

$MRVL acquired Celestial AI in Feb 2026, and Lazovsky became a senior member of $MRVL. This is when the problem started, I think. $MRVL didn't spend $3.25 billion on outsourcing one of the key components in their future business. They wanted a vertically integrated system.

Lazovsky must know the gory details of $POET's optical interposer. And yet, it belongs to $POET. It looks like $MRVL decided to develop their own interposer instead of outsourcing it from $POET. The incident, breach of NDA, was just an excuse, I believe. Because the partnership between $POET and Celestial AI was known for years.

$POET lost an exceptional business opportunity. They need to find another. $MRVL and Lazovsky need to come up with a different solution to make his system work without $POET's interposer. He is a smart guy and he may have an idea already. But will it be different enough for him to be able to avoid an IP battle? $POET looks screwed, $MRVL is getting into a risky business development. But somehow I don't find this will kill $POET, because their optical interposer can work in a different setting and doesn't depend on Lazovsky's fabric. It's the other way around at the moment.

text:
🌟Stocks in Focus 🐾 04/21

■ Strong Movers

$UNH
ニュース：1Q調整後EPSは7.23ドルで予想を66セント上回り、2026年通期の調整後利益見通しを18.25ドル超に引き上げ。
株価：+7.0%。Dowの押し上げ要因として際立った。
ポイント：指数全体は弱かったが、好決算＋ガイダンス引き上げは素直に買われた。

$ALAB
ニュース：Amazon–Anthropic大型契約を受け、AWS向け接続・スイッチ周辺の代表銘柄として物色。Amazonとの間で最大65億ドルの製品購入に連動するワラント契約を持つ点が再評価された。
株価：+9.3%。
ポイント：AIインフラ資金がクラウド本体から、ラック内外の接続・信号整流・光エンジンへ広がっていることを示す動き。

$CRDO
ニュース：4/13にDustPhotonics買収を発表し、SerDes/DSP/Silicon Photonics/システム統合までの垂直統合型接続スタックを構築。FY2027の光関連売上5億ドル超の見通し。
株価：+5.2%。
ポイント：AIクラスタ拡張で必要になる高速接続・光配線側の代表格。

■ Earnings / Guidance

$AAPL
ニュース：Tim Cookが9月1日付で退任し、John TernusがCEOに就任。市場ではAppleのAI戦略がより慎重・漸進的になる可能性が意識された。
株価：-2.5%。当日の象徴的な下落銘柄。
ポイント：単なる人事交代ではなく、AI時代におけるAppleの競争ポジション再評価の色が強い。

■ AI / Data Center / Infrastructure

$AMZN
ニュース：Anthropicに最大250億ドルを追加投資。AnthropicはAWSに今後10年で1000億ドル超を投じ、Trainium2〜4世代を使い年末までに約1GW、最終的に最大5GWの計算能力を確保する計画。
株価：+0.7%。地合い悪化で上値は抑えられた。
ポイント：単なる出資ではなく、クラウド・カスタムAI半導体・電力を巻き込む大型AI需要の長期固定化。

$META
ニュース：オクラホマ州タルサで10億ドル超のAI最適化DC着工。閉ループ液冷を採用し、変電所・送電線を含むグリッドインフラに数億ドル規模を投入。水・下水コストも全額負担。
株価：-0.3%。株価は小幅安だが、AIインフラ建設の事実は強い。
ポイント：AI投資が液冷・水・変電所・地域インフラ整備まで伴う実装局面に入ったことを示す案件。

■ Hidden AI Winners

$SO （Southern Company）
ニュース：DOEが「UPRISE」プログラムで2029年までに既設原発の出力を5GW増強する支援策を公表。Southernは恩恵候補として明示され、2月には総額265億ドルの融資パッケージも発表済み。

ポイント：新設原発は時間がかかる一方、既設原発のuprateはAI向け大口電源の最短ルート。AI電源不足が長引くほど再評価余地がある。

$CAT （Caterpillar）
ニュース：テキサスの送電制約を背景に、データセンター開発企業がオンサイト発電へ傾斜。Caterpillarはギガワット級の天然ガス発電機セットを供給し、初回受注から1年未満で発電開始できる体制。

ポイント：送電網が間に合わないほど、ブリッジ電源としての発電機そのものがAIインフラの必需品に。

$EME （EMCOR）
ニュース：Metaのタルサ案件はピーク時1,000人超の建設雇用。Microsoftも北米建設労組とAI施工人材育成で提携。施工能力そのものがボトルネックとしてテーマ化。

ポイント：AI投資が「発表フェーズ」から「現場で建てるフェーズ」に移るほど、電気工事・機械設備・ミッションクリティカル施工の比重が上がる。

text:
The Invisible Backbone of the MI450: Is This the Ultimate Asymmetric Play?

The silicon gold rush has hit a literal wall: the thermal ceiling.

As we move deeper into 2026, the conversation has shifted from "Who has the fastest chip?" to "Who can keep the chip from melting?" This is where the real asymmetric opportunities are hiding—not in the giants everyone is already talking about, but in the mechanical necessities that make their hardware possible.

The "Transparent Secret" in the Supply Chain

In March, Trio-Tech International $TRT announced a $5.3M order for next-gen AI GPU burn-in boards. While the "leading IDM" remains unnamed, the fingerprints lead to one place: $AMD.

The Geometry: The order is centered in the Singapore/Malaysia corridor—$AMDs primary global hub for back-end testing and packaging.

The Timing: Shipments align perfectly with the massive Q3 2026 rollout of the Instinct MI450.

The Tech: High-power "burn-in" testing is no longer optional for multi-chip architectures using silicon photonics. It is the gatekeeper of reliability.

Why Asymmetry Matters

The matter is now closed and the company has regained compliance with Nasdaq regulations.

text:
While geopolitical tension usually triggers a knee-jerk reaction, there is a strong case to be made that the market has already digested much of this risk. Here is why this surge might not be the "market killer" it seems:
Markets Have "Priced In" the Tension
We have been seeing a military buildup in the Middle East since January, and the S&P 500 has still managed to maintain a remarkably resilient upward channel. History shows that markets are often "forward-looking" and tend to hit "peak uncertainty" before a conflict even reaches its height. We are already seeing Asian and Emerging markets rebound as they shift focus back to corporate fundamentals and the AI rally, suggesting that traders are looking through the noise and betting on an eventual diplomatic resolution.
The "Defense and Tech" Safety Net
The current market is heavily weighted toward defense and high-tech infrastructure. A surge in military activity often acts as a backstop for major aerospace and defense stocks, while the ongoing "Photonics Supercycle" and AI demand remain largely independent of Middle Eastern geography. As long as the earnings of these heavyweights stay strong, the broader indices have a structural floor that wasn't there in previous decades.
Historical Resilience
If you look at the stats, the S&P 500 typically sees a modest dip during the initial impact of military interventions, but in 19 out of 20 major post-WWII cases, the market fully recovered to pre-event levels in less than a month. Even in the middle of "Operation Epic Fury," we have seen markets claw back losses quickly. Investors who panic-sell during these headlines often end up missing the "V-shaped" recovery that follows once the path to a ceasefire or stabilized status quo becomes clear.
The Strategic Buildup as a Catalyst for Peace
While a "surge" sounds aggressive, it is often used as a mechanical lever for leverage in negotiations. With a ceasefire deadline approaching tomorrow, April 22, this show of force is likely the final push to secure a long-term deal in Islamabad. If that happens, we could see a massive "relief rally" that catapults the index toward those 7,600 targets even faster.
The trend is your friend until it ends, and right now, the long-term trend on that weekly chart is still pointing up.

text:
Thoughts on Nynomic ($M7U) at €145M MC:

Caution: this is a numbers-heavy post where I walk through some of my modelling.

No one’s really covered this on X since Nynomic doesn’t disclose subsidiary rev/margins.

TLDR: Nynomic is very cheap at current 1.3x multiples vs metrology peers like $ONTO (10.7x) & $CAMT (13.8x).

For a v. quick summary of the bull thesis:
- Asymmetric exposure to photonics supercycle via their LayTec subsidiary - chokepoint in InP/GaN MOCVD via $AIXA ramp
- $AIXA is 80–90% of global MOCVD

Onto financials…

-> By triangulating data points, I get to ~€15M revenue for LayTec in FY25:

LayTec has ~800 installed reactors globally (based on customer list + historical $AIXA shipments + 25 years of sales)

- New-reactor ASP: €95k / reactor
- New reactors attached/yr (global MOCVD share, $AIXA + part of $VECO): ~100 units
- New sensor revenue: 100 × €95k = ~€9.5M/yr
- Service/software renewals: ~10–12% of installed base annually at €20–30k each = ~€1.5–3M/yr
- Etch endpoint: ~€1–3M/yr

-> Looking fwd to 2026:
$AIXA Q126 orders were €171M at >65% opto mix. Call it €111M of opto equipment orders in a single quarter.

If $AIXA’s FY26 opto revenue runs at ~€350M (conservative 62.5% of the €560M guide), and LayTec attaches at conservative 3–4% of reactor value on new orders:

- That implies €10–14M of incremental LayTec revenue over the 2027–2028 recognition window.

- So LayTec could reach €22–28M in FY27 layered on top of the ~€15M base I walked through above.

Imo, the stock is starting to price in that inflection now.

And consequently gives Nynomic a 1.3x EV/Sales multiple.

That is very cheap against metrology pure-plays but fair against companies like Jenoptik who deal w/ photonics in Europe also.

Imo, Metrology names would be a better/conservative comparison since the Nynomic group is ~80% photonics + ~20% metrology which acts as a drag on the financials.

So I ultimately see Nynomic re-rating towards metrology names at least, like $ONTO/ $CAMT who have multiples of 10.7x & 13.8x.

Especially if LayTec proves materially higher-growth and higher-margin than the wider Nynomic group.

-> I currently hold a position in Nynomic.

No one’s really covered this on X since Nynomic doesn’t disclose subsidiary rev/margins. TLDR: Nynomic is very cheap at current 1.3x multiples vs metrology peers like $ONTO (10.7x) & $CAMT (13.8x). For a v. quick summary of the bull thesis: - Asymmetric exposure to photonics supercycle via their LayTec subsidiary - chokepoint in InP/GaN MOCVD via $AIXA ramp - $AIXA is 80–90% of global MOCVD Onto financials… -> By triangulating data points, I get to ~€15M revenue for LayTec in FY25: LayTec has ~800 installed reactors globally (based on customer list + historical $AIXA shipments + 25 years of sales) - New-reactor ASP: €95k / reactor - New reactors attached/yr (global MOCVD share, $AIXA + part of $VECO): ~100 units - New sensor revenue: 100 × €95k = ~€9.5M/yr - Service/software renewals: ~10–12% of installed base annually at €20–30k each = ~€1.5–3M/yr - Etch endpoint: ~€1–3M/yr -> Looking fwd to 2026: $AIXA Q126 orders were €171M at >65% opto mix. Call it €111M of opto equipment orders in a single quarter. If $AIXA’s FY26 opto revenue runs at ~€350M (conservative 62.5% of the €560M guide), and LayTec attaches at conservative 3–4% of reactor value on new orders: - That implies €10–14M of incremental LayTec revenue over the 2027–2028 recognition window. - So LayTec could reach €22–28M in FY27 layered on top of the ~€15M base I walked through above. Imo, the stock is starting to price in that inflection now. And consequently gives Nynomic a 1.3x EV/Sales multiple. That is very cheap against metrology pure-plays but fair against companies like Jenoptik who deal w/ photonics in Europe also. Imo, Metrology names would be a better/conservative comparison since the Nynomic group is ~80% photonics + ~20% metrology which acts as a drag on the financials. So I ultimately see Nynomic re-rating towards metrology names at least, like $ONTO/ $CAMT who have multiples of 10.7x & 13.8x. Especially if LayTec proves materially higher-growth and higher-margin than the wider Nynomic group. -> I currently hold a position in Nynomic."}}}, "rest_id": "2046699141732974659", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "177626", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$LPK $LPKF 🔥
This company is not only successful in the laser and photonics sector, but also active in the solar and quantum computing space, where it collaborates with companies such as Volkswagen AG and Infineon Technologies AG.

The market cap of this company appears extremely undervalued, especially considering that its patented LIDE technology for glass substrates has massive game-changing potential.

In my view, the current valuation doesn’t even come close to reflecting its true potential.

text:
As of April 23, 2026

The AI Photonics and Optical Infrastructure sector is showing a sharply bifurcated tape today, with large-cap AI infrastructure names holding green while speculative small and micro-cap photonics names unwind hard, the top loser being $IQE down 20.53%. The move reflects a parabolic blow-off on the London line (IQE.L ran over 107% in the two weeks into April 17 before rolling over), with the OTC mirror IQEPF gapping down today on profit-taking and zero fresh fundamental news into the May 20 earnings print (https://t.co/udYbhT79LN).

The quality leadership is sending a clear signal: capital is rotating out of speculative substrate and early-stage PIC names into the picks-and-shovels plays with visible AI capex pull-through. $MRVL leads at +5.24% on the Amazon-Anthropic $100B deal tailwind layered on top of the Google custom AI chip talks and Nvidia's $2B NVLink Fusion investment, which together anchor Marvell as the default optical DSP and custom ASIC partner across every hyperscaler (https://t.co/2jwCYbswdK).

$MRVL: 5.24%
$VIAV: 4.15%
$TER: 4.10%
$CIEN: 3.38%
$KEYS: 2.23%
$GFS: 1.90%
$FORM: 1.50%
$GLW: 0.44%
$BESIY: -0.21%
$SPX: -0.41%
$NDX: -0.57%
$VICR: -1.84%
$AEHR: -1.90%
$ATEYY: -2.01%
$TSEM: -2.34%
$LITE: -3.06%
$COHR: -3.65%
$SLOIF: -6.97%
$SIVEF: -7.78%
$AAOI: -7.82%
$POET: -8.22%
$LWLG: -10.29%
$AXTI: -13.42%
$XNDU: -16.98%
$IQE: -20.53%

1. Optical Transceivers & Active Networking
$CIEN, $GLW: The large-cap optical layer held up on AI fabric demand, with Ciena catching a bid as 800G and 1.6T buildouts continue into 2H26 and Corning benefiting from the optical cable pull-through.
$LITE, $COHR: Both names gave back modestly after a multi-week run, looking like position-trimming rather than thesis breakdown ahead of earnings season.
$AAOI: Down 7.82%, this is the sharpest move among the transceiver names and reads as profit-taking on the recent domestic-manufacturing tariff-exemption bid plus microcap beta to the broader sector unwind.

2. Compound Semiconductor Substrates & Foundries
$GFS, $TSEM: Foundries split, with GlobalFoundries up on SiPho and advanced node traction while Tower gave back some of its recent run on sector risk-off.
$AXTI, $IQE, $SLOIF, $SIVEF: This is where the damage concentrated. AXTI down 13.42%, IQE down 20.53%, Soitec down 6.97%, SiTime down 7.78%. InP substrate demand remains the bottleneck thesis intact, but the OTC liquidity and parabolic-unwind dynamics are doing the driving today, not any fundamental deterioration.
$BESIY: Flat on the day, advanced packaging and hybrid bonding exposure providing some ballast.

3. Early-Stage Photonic Materials & PICs
$POET, $LWLG, $XNDU: All three got hit hard (POET -8.22%, LWLG -10.29%, XNDU -16.98%) in what looks like a coordinated speculative unwind. Cramer's measured POET comments on CNBC this morning didn't help sentiment on the name, and the broader early-stage PIC basket tends to move as one when risk comes off. Thesis remains long-dated optionality, but the tape is punishing anything that ran too far too fast.

4. Semiconductor Equipment, Test & Infrastructure
$TER, $KEYS, $FORM, $VIAV: The test and measurement complex was the cleanest trade on the board today, with Teradyne, Keysight, FormFactor, and Viavi all green on the read-through from strong AI infrastructure capex guidance. This is where the sector's earnings visibility is most concrete.
$MRVL, $ATEYY: Marvell up 5.24% as the clear AI infrastructure beneficiary, while Advantest gave back a bit on what appears to be profit-taking after a strong run.
$AEHR, $VICR: Both down modestly, with AEHR's move tracking the broader SiC and compound semi test cycle concern rather than company-specific news.

The AI Photonics and Optical Infrastructure complex went risk-on across the board, led by a 10%+ rip in $SLOIF after Bloomberg crowned Soitec the best-performing European stock of 2026, up more than 300% YTD with an 84% gain this month alone , and Oddo BHF raised its target to €85 from €50 (https://t.co/EbZP37PIaz). SPX +1.20% and NDX +1.29% gave the tape a clean risk-on backdrop.
Photonics has moved from optionality to consensus theme. Hyperscaler capex is flowing through transceivers into PICs and the upstream substrate layer, and the market is now hunting picks-and-shovels exposure with structural moats. Today's laggards are mostly OTC liquidity friction or single-name overhang, not thesis breaks.

$IQE: -11.09%
1. Compound Semi Substrates & Foundries
SLOIF, IQE, AXTI, SIVEF: The substrate layer is where the photonics money is being priced. SLOIF parabolic on Bloomberg coverage and the Oddo target hike. IQE down 11% on an insider sale headline, with Andrew Nelson dumping roughly £1.5M of stock at £0.49 per share, the biggest IQE insider sale in a year Simply Wall St, classic profit-taking on the YTD leader. AXTI continues to act as the cleaner US-listed InP proxy. SIVEF whipped on OTC liquidity, not fundamentals.
GFS, TSEM: Foundry leverage to the photonics buildout. TSEM is the silicon photonics workhorse for Tower's CPO ramp, GFS riding the broader AI custom silicon wave.

2. Optical Transceivers, DSP Silicon & Active Networking
COHR, CIEN, AAOI, LITE, MRVL: 800G/1.6T transceiver demand still the cleanest hyperscaler read-through. COHR leading today, MRVL strong on merchant DSP silicon read-through, AAOI holding bid on the 1.6T order book, LITE quiet after the Nvidia investment digestion phase.
GLW, VIAV: GLW slightly red on profit-taking after its run. VIAV ripping +7% as network test demand benefits from the same buildout.

3. Equipment, Test & Packaging
FORM, AEHR, TER, KEYS: Wafer-level test and burn-in picks-and-shovels trade. FORM and AEHR both up sharp on continued read-through from HBM and AI accelerator volume. TER and KEYS round out the test exposure.
ATEYY, BESIY: ATEYY (Aixtron) is the MOCVD tool play for compound semi growth, shares more than doubled in 2026. BESIY is the hybrid bonding leverage to advanced packaging and CPO.

4. Early-Stage Photonic Materials & PICs
LWLG: Modest green on EO polymer optionality, still pre-revenue, sentiment-driven.
POET, XNDU: Both red. POET digesting after recent rally. XNDU off 7.5% on profit-taking after a parabolic run on Nvidia's Ising quantum AI catalyst, trading at extreme price-to-sales on roughly $4.6M revenue and a $70.7M net loss Invezz, classic mean-reversion in the speculative bucket."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2044554804744360306"}}, "rest_id": "2045355011358802237", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "34160", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2047447518049649062", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "20530", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Photonic computing for edge AI is one of those stories that sounds niche until you think about energy.

If NeuraWave delivers faster inference with lower power draw, that matters anywhere AI has to live outside giant data centres.

#EdgeAI #Photonics #AIHardware

Anders Storm’s repeated, specific praise is a strong and credible counter to blanket “pumper” accusations against Serenity (@aleabitoreddit). @stormdirac 

Storm is not a random retail account: He was CEO of Sivers for 8–10 years (built the photonics side). He knows the tech, partnerships (Ayar Labs, Jabil, etc.), and execution realities better than almost anyone outside the current management. 

He’s not hyping blindly - he engages with Serenity’s threads on supply-chain mapping and calls them “great deep research.”

BUT we must not forget the bigger picture: Marvell still desperately needs a reliable light source (lasers + External Light Sources / ELS). Polariton only provides the modulator (the “switch” that turns the light on and off). It does NOT provide the actual light itself. This is exactly where Ayar Labs + Sivers come into play. Ayar Labs is significantly more advanced than most competitors with its mature SuperNova platform — already in advanced prototyping, with proven system-level integration and strong hyperscaler interest. Ayar’s SuperNova platform uses Sivers’ high-performance multi-wavelength laser arrays as the core light engine. Why Ayar Labs is significantly better positioned than most competitors: Its SuperNova platform is one of the most mature CPO/ELS solutions on the market. Already in advanced prototyping with strong hyperscaler interest. Recently dropped Lumentum and Macom to keep Sivers as its exclusive laser supplier — a major vote of confidence. BUT competition is here : Lumentum ($LITE) and Coherent ($COHR) → already nearly fully booked through 2027-2028. Smaller players like Casela, Scintil Photonics and Xscape → solid but limited in scale and capacity. Bottom line: The POET drama is hurting short-term sentiment and creating volatility across the entire photonic AI sector. However, the core $SIVE thesis remains fully intact. Jabil’s 1.6T LRO partnership (the big industrial manufacturing arm) is completely separate and unaffected. Ayar Labs stays very well positioned, and O-Net is also active. text:
Ever since I started covering photonics...

My comments were full of "$POET, $POET, $POET"

WHY? Me nor a single photonics trader I know ever thought anything of it.

Pre rev. Paid stock promo. Now wrecked trying to hype the stock then getting $MRVL mad.

Be careful buying a stock in a hot category just because "it hasn't ran yet". That's not an investment thesis.

Here’s the landscape: $MTSI – Builds high-performance analog chips that help move data efficiently inside networks $GLW – Makes the fiber and glass that physically carries data across the world $CIEN – Provides networking gear that powers high-speed data traffic $COHR – Develops lasers and photonics components used in AI and data center systems $FN – Manufactures optical hardware that connects and scales data centers $MRVL – Designs custom chips that act as the backbone for cloud and AI infrastructure $ALAB – Focused on connectivity chips solving data flow bottlenecks in AI clusters $AVGO – Supplies critical semis across networking, storage, and AI infrastructure $AEHR – Builds testing equipment for next-gen semiconductors and photonics devices $CRDO – Creates ultra-fast interconnect chips linking AI servers at scale $AAOI – Produces optical modules that enable high-speed data transmission $LITE – Designs optical components and lasers powering AI data center connectivity This isn’t just another AI theme. It’s the plumbing behind it. text:
On a day where data centers from $CIFR to $NBIS & photonics from $POET to $AAOI were pulling back strongly

Might make you think we're risk-off?

Except the rare earth/critical metals sector is greener than uranium

$USAR hit another +7% today erasing all of Friday gains as a sympathy play to $CRML's new acquisition

Can't believe it was trading for what the government paid for it last week.

text:
For long-term investments (3-5+ years) in photonics/AI data links, focus on proven moats + execution over pure hype:

1. **MRVL**: Best balance—custom AI silicon + optical interconnects, real hyperscaler revenue.
2. **TSM**: Unmatched scale as the foundry powering it all.
3. **AVGO**: Networking dominance with optical edge.
4. **ASML**: Lithography monopoly for advanced chips.

High-upside but riskier: POET/AAOI if their tech scales. AXTI/AEHR solid but more niche. DYOR, diversify.

text:
Photonics stocks ride AI's need for faster, cooler data links. All have tailwinds, but vary in moat, execution risk, and hype vs reality.

Research priority (upside + strategic edge first):

1. POET (+113% PT): Optical interposers could transform chip comms—highest reward/risk.
2. MRVL (+76%): Custom AI silicon + optics; proven hyperscaler traction.
3. AAOI (+68%): GPU transceivers; direct AI cluster play.
4. AXTI (+71%): Indium phosphide wafers; foundational material.
5. AEHR (+65%): Burn-in testing; scales reliability at volume.

Start here, then TSM/INTC/ASML as anchors. Smaller ones like LWLG/POET need deeper DD on tech viability.

text:
The 7-Layer Photonics Stack

1.Materials & Wafers — InP, GaAs, silicon substrates. InP supply is the chokepoint.

https://t.co/X8pBSexGF9 & Epitaxy — Equipment to grow the crystal layers. MOCVD machines. Few players.

3.Lasers & Devices — VCSELs, DFB lasers, modulators. Where the photons actually get made.

4.Foundries (III-V Fabs) — The fabs that process compound semiconductors. Scarce and specialized.

5.Test & Inspection — Verify everything works at speed and scale. Lower binary risk, often overlooked.

6.Optics & Modules — Transceivers, MEMS, co-packaged optics (CPO). Where hyperscaler demand hits first.

7.Networking & Connectivity — Optical switches, DSPs, interconnect. The system layer.

text:
Ich habe die Liste geklaut, aber einfach um sicher zu gehen, dass auch in der deutschen Bubble ein paar Leute vom Thema #Photonic mitbekommen.

THE 7 LAYERS OF PHOTONICS

1. Materials & Wafers (substrate layer)
$AXTI, $IQE $WOLF, $COHR, $LWLG

2. Tools (fabrication layer)
$ASML, $AMAT, $LRCX 

3. Lasers (light generation layer)
$LITE, $COHR, $LASR, $SMTC 

4. Foundries (manufacturing layer)
$TSM, $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, $INTC

5. Test, Inspection & Packaging (reliability layer)
$AEHR, $VIAV, $ONTO, $AMKR, $FN

6. Optics (module layer)
$AAOI, $POET, $GLW

7. Networking (connectivity layer)
$CRDO, $MRVL, $AVGO, $ANET, $CIEN

text:
c:7

$M7U (Nynomic AG) is a German micro-cap, off the beaten path, directly part of the photonics supply chain.

Here’s my simplified explanation of what this company does and why I like it:

AI datacenters need massive amounts of fiber optic connections to move data between chips at the speed of light. Those fiber optic components are manufactured inside specialized reactors made by a company called AIXTRON – which controls roughly 90% of the global market for these machines.

Nynomic owns LayTec – a company that makes the sensors that sit inside every single one of those AIXA reactors and measure what’s happening in real time during production. Without them, manufacturing yields drop dramatically. Near 1:1 attach rate on every reactor AIXA ships.

AIXA’s optics segment is their primary growth engine for 2026, guided at €520M revenue. LayTec rides that wave on every single order.

Now the valuation gap compared to its nearest competitors. $ONTO trades at ~12.7x sales. $CAMT trades at ~15x sales. Nynomic trades at ~1.5x sales. 

The market is currently treating it like a struggling industrial company because of the 2025 restructuring noise – but management guides for significant recovery in 2026 with disproportionately large profit expansion. Medium-term target is €200M revenue at 16-19% margins.

Given the current market cap, hitting those targets would likely force a massive re-rating toward the 3x–5x sales range typical for European specialized semi-cap players.

You’re essentially buying a hidden monopoly inside the hottest semiconductor supply chain in the world at a steep discount – before it has had a chance to run.

This is a position I opened on $IBKR last week. Not available on $HOOD. Looking to add more this week if the price dips or stays flat a little longer.

text:
🎧 MARKET RECAP — APRIL 21
“Textbook bull trap… and they slammed the door into the close.”

Wild day… and a nasty close.

$SPY -0.7% opened strong 📈
➡️ Momentum looked real early
➡️ Then everything flipped hard

🧠 WHAT HAPPENED
• Midday weakness hit during Fed Chair Senate hearing
• Confidence faded → sellers stepped in
• Late-day pressure accelerated after headlines around JD Vance / geopolitics

➡️ End result: full risk-off into the close

⚡ DATA CENTERS / AI INFRA
$IREN -7.2%
$CIFR -6.6%
➡️ Early strength → sold off with everything else
➡️ Still one of the hottest narratives in the market
➡️ Pullbacks likely getting watched for re-entry

🔥 SHORT SQUEEZE MADNESS
$CAR +16.8%
➡️ Halted MULTIPLE times
➡️ Still ripping
➡️ Float dynamics + positioning = chaos
💡 This is peak squeeze behavior
➡️ Not fundamentals — pure positioning + momentum

🚀 OTHER HOT POCKETS
SPACE (still volatile)
$ASTS -1.2%
$RKLB -3.1%
$SIDU -13.5%
➡️ Big swings continue
➡️ Retail still heavily involved

PHOTONICS / AI DATA FLOW
$AXTI -4.8%
$AAOI -7.9%
$CRDO +5.0%
➡️ Quietly one of the strongest themes lately
➡️ Still seeing rotation interest

🚗 EYES ON TOMORROW
$TSLA earnings
➡️ Major catalyst for tech
➡️ Could drive direction short-term

⚠️ BIG PICTURE
• Market tried to break higher
• Failed
• Sold hard into close

➡️ That’s not bullish short-term

💡 Key question now:
Is this just a dip…
or the start of a deeper pullback? Market is recovering AFTERHOURS so time will tell.

text:
$IONQ
The leader in quantum computing - Quantum computing is important because it uses quantum mechanical principles to solve complex problems—such as molecular simulation, advanced optimization, and breaking modern encryption—exponentially faster than classical computers. It promises to revolutionize industries by unlocking up to billion in value through faster drug discovery, improved logistics, and AI advancement. **IBM, RGTI, QBTS, INFQ** <-- are also some of the best in quantum. 

Key Technology and Cloud Partners
- NVIDIA: Collaborating on hybrid quantum-HPC technologies and integrating IonQ hardware with NVIDIA's CUDA-Q platform.
- Amazon Braket: Cloud access provider.
- Microsoft Azure Quantum: Cloud access provider.
- Google Cloud: Cloud access provider.
- Dell: Collaboration on integrating quantum systems.
- NKT Photonics: Partner for developing next-generation laser systems

Automotive and Logistics Partners
- Hyundai Motor Company: Focused on 3D point cloud analysis for autonomous vehicles and battery chemistry simulations.
- Toyota Tsusho: Expanding quantum computing solutions in Japan.
- Einride: Partner for optimizing freight mobility with quantum technology.

Defense and Aerospace Partners
- Lockheed Martin: Early investor and partner in trapped-ion technology.
- Airbus: Partner in aerospace applications.
- General Dynamics Information Technology (GDIT): Developing quantum solutions for government customers.
- U.S. Air Force Research Lab (AFRL): Research partnerships for quantum networking and algorithm development.
- Heven AeroTech: Developing quantum-enabled drones for national security. 

Life Sciences, Finance, and Other Partners
- AstraZeneca: Collaborating on quantum applications in pharmaceuticals.
- CCRM (Centre for Commercialization of Regenerative Medicine): Accelerating therapeutic development using quantum-AI.
- Goldman Sachs: Exploring financial applications.
- GE Research: Partner for industrial applications.
- Accenture: Collaborating to accelerate quantum adoption into the enterprise.
- Q-CTRL: Partner for quantum control software.

With high growth projections and a phenomenal spread sheet this stock can realistically see $100 with the AI boom. They're growing exponentially and can see this continuing into 2030. They estimated last Q revenue at $40.39 and beat by 78.49% at $61.89M. They're estimating next quarter to be $49.73M and I expect them to exceed that. They went from 7 to 8 figure growth in less then a year. Pay attention to continued partners and expectations on growth. This company IPO'd only at the end of 2021.

text:
Nano-biology and chemistry, immune system, materials, Feynman’s lectures, embryonic development all the way from DNA, how everyday things like iPhones work all the way down, causality, agency, photonics, economics, holographic cosmology,  … 

Thanks for the amazing work you do! It’s truly inspiring

@tim_cook 6G fiber optics, Wi-Fi 8, and cutting-edge technology are available in the United States, but beyond electronics and computing, research should be conducted into photonic quantum computing and silicon photonics to create software and hardware that improve global sustainability.

text:
$LPK / $LPKF

Getting a blessing from the godfather of FinX is both heaven sent and a curse. If the fundamentals are solid, catalysts are ripe, and macro supports it, you’re golden. If not, it’s a pump heading straight to oblivion.

I first came across this name talking with Serenity back in January. One of those quiet asymmetric setups that just hadn’t had its moment yet.

LPKF is a chokepoint for glass core substrates via LIDE technology. 80%+ of major global players chose their equipment for process validation. Partners include Samsung, Intel, Corning, and more. FWD P/E of 11-12.5x for 2027 dropping to ~7.8x in 2028, clean balance sheet, ~3.8% debt to equity, no dilution overhang.

The photonics re-rating is happening in real time, and stocks that deserve it are getting found. $LPKF has to prove itself here.

text:
The recent strong rally in Credo Technology (CRDO) was sparked by its April 14 announcement acquiring DustPhotonics for ~$1.2B (cash + stock + earnouts). This adds silicon photonics tech for 800G/1.6T+ optical transceivers, expanding beyond active electrical cables into faster AI data center interconnects—targeting $500M+ optical revenue by FY27.

It built on Jefferies' fresh Buy rating, explosive AI-driven growth (200%+ YoY revenue), and bullish order flow signals. Stock up ~46% in the past 5 days as of April 23.

@Thomas_james_1 $LITE near $850 vs $SIVE at $3 — valuation divergence in photonics: fundamentals or market cap effects? Small-cap photonics names offer higher beta in upcycles but with higher volatility — option spreads may work better than outright shares.

text:
Leeway AI: STM pivots to data centers while automotive stalls and politics cloud the room
$STM

STMicroelectronics started mass production of silicon photonics chips for AI data centers in Q1 2026 and expanded its 800-volt DC power portfolio alongside NVIDIA, while deepening ties with AWS to strengthen its position in AI infrastructure.

The company completed its acquisition of NXP's MEMS business, expected to contribute mid-range revenue in the $40 million area over time, with particular strength in automotive and industrial sensors.

In China, STM began series production of its STM32 H7 microcontroller family, with plans to extend to H5 and C5 variants, improving supply chain resilience against geopolitical pressure.The momentum came against headwinds in Q4 2025. A net loss of $30 million, impairment charges and restructuring costs totaling $141 million, and a 31.6% revenue decline in the power and discrete segment weighed heavily on results.

Management is budgeting $2 to $2.2 billion in capital expenditure for 2026, mostly directed toward SiC and 300-millimeter capacity expansion.The company sees normalization of customer inventory levels by the end of Q2 2026 as the critical threshold for sustainable recovery.

"Leeway AI: STM pivots to data centers while automotive stalls and politics cloud the room $STM STMicroelectronics started mass production of silicon photonics chips for AI data centers in Q1 2026 and expanded its 800-volt DC power portfolio alongside NVIDIA, while deepening ties with AWS to strengthen its position in AI infrastructure. The company completed its acquisition of NXP's MEMS business, expected to contribute mid-range revenue in the $40 million area over time, with particular strength in automotive and industrial sensors. In China, STM began series production of its STM32 H7 microcontroller family, with plans to extend to H5 and C5 variants, improving supply chain resilience against geopolitical pressure.The momentum came against headwinds in Q4 2025. A net loss of $30 million, impairment charges and restructuring costs totaling $141 million, and a 31.6% revenue decline in the power and discrete segment weighed heavily on results. "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "33296", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2048853695010365707", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "784", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
The CPU bottleneck could be bigger than photonics.

Because it only gets worse as AI chips get better.

A Georgia Tech/$INTC paper makes it clear. The researchers hooked up real agentic AI workloads (research assistants, coding agents, web-search agents) and stopped to watch every step.

On a cheaper GPU setup, the CPU caused 38% of the total wait time. Then they ran the same workloads on an H200. The CPU's share jumped to 65%.

A faster GPU just means you finish the GPU computations sooner, which makes the CPU wait for all the agentic actions look even bigger by comparison.

Every accelerator upgrade only sends buyers back to order more CPUs...

This is exactly why $INTC CFO said unfulfilled demand for CPUs starts with a B.

More deep research into the CPU bottleneck in me and @KawzInvests Substack deep dive, really worked to help folks wrap their head around it in a digestible format.

$AMD $INTC $ARM $QCOM $TSM

Because it only gets worse as AI chips get better. A Georgia Tech/$INTC paper makes it clear. The researchers hooked up real agentic AI workloads (research assistants, coding agents, web-search agents) and stopped to watch every step. On a cheaper GPU setup, the CPU caused 38% of the total wait time. Then they ran the same workloads on an H200. The CPU's share jumped to 65%. A faster GPU just means you finish the GPU computations sooner, which makes the CPU wait for all the agentic actions look even bigger by comparison. Every accelerator upgrade only sends buyers back to order more CPUs... This is exactly why $INTC CFO said unfulfilled demand for CPUs starts with a B. Blood pressure is one step. But the real prize is glucose monitoring. A future where millions of people no longer have to rely on needles, strips, and constant friction to stay on top of their health. A future where wearables help monitor the body quietly, continuously, in the background. And the opportunity goes beyond that. Not just blood pressure. Not just glucose.  Potentially hydration, recovery, respiratory-related signals, temperature-linked changes, and even sensing different things in the air around us. My own view: I think this launches in 2028. And if that’s right, pre-series orders 12–18 months before launch would make a lot of sense. Now imagine Apple mass-producing health-focused consumer hardware at 50M+ units per year. That’s not just massive for Sivers.  That’s massive for the future of healthcare. "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "33296", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2048514663634108784", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "211", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Bottlenecks are the theme of 2026.

We’ve already had:

→ memory bottlenecks
→ photonics bottlenecks
→ CPU bottlenecks
→ GPU bottlenecks

But the next question is bigger:
What powers it all?

AI data centres don’t just need chips.
They need electricity at insane scale.

My power watchlist:

→ $CEG for nuclear
→ $VST for power generation
→ $TLN for nuclear/data centre deals
→ $GEV for turbines + grid equipment
→ $ETN for electrical infrastructure
→ $PWR for grid buildout
→ $OKLO / $SMR for SMRs

The next AI bottleneck will not be inside the data centre.
It will be the power needed to run it.

text:
$LWLG

Don’t miss this one. A pullback soon back could happen. 

This looks a lot like buying $COHR before photonics became a core AI data center bottleneck theme.

AI photonics new age is here. If this sticks, this one could be huge. 

Revenue growth Q: +594%
Gross profit growth Q: +619%
~$69M current assets
~$66M net cash position
~8450 PS ratio 

(reflects extremely early revenue base, not mature scaling)

Still in commercialization / validation phase for EO polymer photonics

This is like buying $COHR at 15$ in 2014

All at a 2B market cap. 

We saw it with $LITE and $COHR — optical infrastructure compounds hard once AI data center demand shifts from narrative to structural bottleneck.

That’s the same structural lane where photonics becomes the invisible layer of compute scaling.

Not saying $LWLG becomes $COHR.

This is where those asymmetries live.

text:
First time I see someone on my feed recommending $AIP. It's nice to see different stocks than the traditional photonics / 800G plays that everybody hypes every day.

I'm also quite bullish on $AIP, although I sold my shares for a quick profit. What I really like is that they are actively decelerating their China business. It accounted for 25-30% before, which was a massive geopolitical headwind.

The downside for me is the royalty timeline. Those revenues only materialize a couple of years after the licensing deal is signed. So while I really like the stock, I'm not sure if it is the best timing to hold right now, but we'll see. The market reflects future potential, not current value so very possible it could go higher in the near term propulsed by INTC and AMD

text:
This work incorporates the Mie-resonant phase-change nanoparticles (vanadium dioxide or VO2) into optical tweezers technology, which brings the ability to manipulate optical forces between attractive & repulsive. W/ biomolecules bonded to the outer surfaces, the phase-change nanoparticles can be utilized as a carrier to dynamically sort the molecules on the surface. Besides, in most of single-molecule experiments, a single molecule is attached to the surface of polystyrene or silica particles, acting as a handle to manipulate & study single molecules by optical force. 

Furthermore, the phase-change nanoparticles can be modified to have a transition temperature as low as 30 ℃ by inducing strain in the material. So the nanoparticles can also act as an all-optical power limiter at the nanoscale: the nanoparticle leaves the tweezer once critical power level is exceeded to protect the biomolecules & cells from heating damage.

https://t.co/RLLDwy6vnL

We present deterministic control over the sign of optical forces exerted on a metasurface integrated on a suspended silicon nanomembrane. By tailoring the geometry of the constituent meta-atoms, we engineer the coherent superposition of their multipolar modes, & consequently, the net optical force experienced by the metasurface wi/in a phase-controlled optical standing wave. This approach establishes a powerful & versatile platform for optical force engineering, w/ potential extensions to optical torques. 

The experimental demonstration of multipolar optical force control holds relevance for diverse apps, including the minimization of photodamage in biological systems, resonant nanoparticle sorting, optical manipulation for precise nanostructure assembly, steering of metavehicles for drug delivery, optically reconfigurable photonic devices, near-surface trapping, & optomechanics in general.

https://t.co/Q88526Olyv

Here we present an approach for the 3D measurement of nanoscale optical forces & torques. This is achieved thru the optical trapping & precision spatial tracking of a designed microscale structure that contains embedded target nanostructures. 

Our method enables the confinement & measurement of nanostructure positions & orientations across three translational & three rotational degrees of freedom, independent of the size, shape & material of the nanostructure. Using this method, we observe transverse optical torque on plasmon-resonant nanostructures & reveal that this behaviour is governed by the optical helicity rather than the angular momentum of incident light.

This versatile platform advances our fundamental understanding of nano-optomechanical interactions & opens up possibilities for precise optical manipulation & nanoactuator design.

https://t.co/1WAO4Ri1Vo

The idea is simple: place the nanostructure to be studied at the center of a tiny, cross-shaped platform, or “micro-drone.” Then use 4 laser beams to hold the platform in place, like invisible tweezers gripping its corners. By carefully tracking how the platform moves & rotates, researchers can then infer the forces acting on the nanostructure inside.

The team tested their approach using tiny gold structures shaped like the letter “V.” Using the new method, when these structures were placed inside the platform & illuminated w/ light, they exhibited an unusual behavior known as transverse optical torque, meaning that instead of twisting themselves along the direction of light, they rotated sideways.

Scientists had predicted that such a twisting effect would be controlled by the light’s angular momentum. But instead, the researchers found that it depends on a more subtle property called optical helicity—a measure of the “handedness,” or twist of the light’s electromagnetic field. They showed this by designing experiments that canceled the light’s angular momentum while preserving its helicity. The sideways torque still remained, confirming that helicity plays the dominant role.

text:
Great request! 15 bottlenecks is a ton—can't fit full top 5-10 lists + prices here without a mega-thread. Quick starters (prices fluctuate; verify live):

HBM/advanced memory: MU (Micron), 005930.KS (Samsung), 000660.KS (SK Hynix), LRCX (Lam Research).

Silicon photonics: LITE (Lumentum), COHR (Coherent), INTC (Intel).

Advanced packaging: AMKR (Amkor), AMAT (Applied Materials), TSM (TSMC).

Liquid cooling: VRT (Vertiv).

Which one to expand on first?

This list is also a capital allocation map.
Every item on it has a company racing to dominate it. Glass core substrates: Corning and AGC. Silicon photonics: Intel and Ayar Labs. HBM: SK Hynix just started mass production for Nvidia's Vera Rubin platform. Grid transformers: 4-year lead times, no quick fix.
The bottleneck that actually determines the AI race timeline isn't compute. It's the thing at the bottom of this list. Thermal management. You can build the chip. You cannot cool the datacenter fast enough.
Which of these are you watching?

text:
Here is an outline of POETs CFO interview on StockTwits and why it is so bullish for $SIVE / $SIVEF:

Thomas Mika on the light source market:

“Fewer competitors. Unique products. Potentially a much larger market than even the high-speed transceiver market.” That’s describing the InP laser layer.

You already know who is sitting inside that layer $SIVE / $SIVEF

POET uses InP-based light sources. 
Celestial AI (now Marvell) runs on external laser supply. The Blazar architecture literally requires what Sivers makes: high-bandwidth, multi-frequency, high-power CW sources.

He also confirmed:
+ PO from Marvell, shipping this year
+ Celestial AI invoice, shipping next quarter
+ Pipeline “far in excess” of the $5M 800G order

Every one of those design wins is a demand signal for the InP laser layer upstream. The more POET scales, the more InP laser supply it pulls through and Sivers is ready to meet that demand.

In short, POET is building the photonic fabric. But fabric needs light. And the light comes from Sivers.

Thomas Mika on the light source market:

“Fewer competitors. Unique products. Potentially a much larger market than even the high-speed transceiver market.” That’s describing the InP laser layer.

You already know who is sitting inside that layer $SIVE / $SIVEF

POET uses InP-based light sources. 
Celestial AI (now Marvell) runs on external laser supply. The Blazar architecture literally requires what Sivers makes: high-bandwidth, multi-frequency, high-power CW sources.

He also confirmed:
+ PO from Marvell, shipping this year
+ Celestial AI invoice, shipping next quarter
+ Pipeline “far in excess” of the $5M 800G order

Every one of those design wins is a demand signal for the InP laser layer upstream. The more POET scales, the more InP laser supply it pulls through and Sivers is ready to meet that demand.

In short, POET is building the photonic fabric. But fabric needs light. POET up 20%+ on 5x normal volume
0:33
Welcome into StockTwits TV. Let’s talk about POET Technologies. They produce semiconductor chips and developed a platform that combines electronics and optics onto a single chip.

0:11
And what a day they had on Monday.
Thomas Mika, their CFO, joins me now.

0:21
Yeah, and you’ve got a lot to talk about. Stock up over 20% on 5x normal volume on Monday.
Now, we have to address the elephant in the room here.

Chapter 2: The Wolfpack Research short report — biggest error explained

0:33
You know, the Wolfpack report last week, and the company sort of addressed those tax concerns by moving the HQ to the United States.
But give it to us straight. What’s the single biggest error in your view in that Wolfpack report that investors were miscalculating?

0:51
Well, I think they called us a promote company, which we’re not.

We’re a real company with real products.

I just don’t think they understand that in the technology space, it takes a lot for a company to get started and face the challenges that we have faced over the course of the past 10 years.
But we’re here now, and we’ve got a lot of interest in our products, we’ve got a pipeline, and we have revenue coming in this year.

So I think the Wolfpack article came the day before tax day, and I think it scared a lot of people unnecessarily because the bottom line with that is that since we were in a net loss position, there was nothing to declare for our U.S. shareholders.

Chapter 3: Message to shareholders who sold on the report
1:33
So it was a big nothingburger, and I think the reaction of the market shows that.

So what’s your message to the shareholders who sold and who saw that report and believed it?

1:52
Well, I hate shorts. I don’t discriminate. I think they’re all maggots.

We’ve worked very hard to build a real company, and I think alarming shareholders right prior to tax day was typical of shorts, but not a very good thing to do frankly.

So how much higher are you versus pre-report?

Chapter 4: Stock price recovery — where POET stands vs. pre-report

2:21
We were trading at about $7.25, came down about a dollar, had recovered somewhat, and then we’re back up considerably today.
I think we’re trading in the $8.50 range.

So we’re up about a dollar from where we were at the time of that report.

Is there a single data point in your last earnings release that you felt didn’t get as much attention as you would have liked?
3:00
No, I don’t think so.

I think most of our shareholders understand where we are from a technology point of view and where we are in terms of developing the company.

We’ve got a lot of steps to go through in this business.

We are attracting attention from the kind of customers that we’ve always wanted to have, which were the hyperscalers who buy modules.

Chapter 5: What to watch in last earnings — hyperscaler attraction
3:26
We’re a component supplier to module makers.

Our chips are high-speed 800G and 1.6T products that go into modules, the transceivers that turn electrical impulses into light impulses.

We’re also in the light source business, which addresses the market for the very advanced companies that are dealing with the issue of converting all of that electrical communication between a GPU and a memory device into light-based communication.

Chapter 6: 800G & 1.6T transceivers + the blue-ocean light source market

4:17
That market is more blue ocean for us.
We’ve got fewer competitors in it.

We’ve got really unique products in it.

It’s potentially a much larger market than even the high-speed transceiver market is.

That’s interesting.

You’re sitting on about $430 million in cash. That’s correct.

In this environment, that’s a pretty big war chest.
Can you walk me through the return on that capital? Is the idea to build a moat through acquisitions, or is that cash a safety net for the company?

Chapter 7: $430M cash — capacity, capability & credibility strategy

5:07
What we’ve said about it is that it gives us three C’s.

It gives us capacity, which we have to build to demonstrate that we can manufacture tens of thousands of devices per month, up to hundreds of thousands of devices per month.

It gives us capability, which is the ability to bring in our own employees and add to our employee base, which we’ve been doing very rapidly in the past quarter.
And it gives us credibility.

It’s really important for our customers to know that we’re going to be there for the long term, and with a lower amount of cash they might conclude otherwise.

So we’re happy with deploying the cash for both organic growth and inorganic growth.

In terms of a moat, we already have a moat. It’s our technology itself.

What we are interested in doing with our capital for acquisitions, or our currency in our stock, is to bring in capability and to own components.

Because in this game of producing modules, almost all of the competitors in our business stack margins.

They buy external components and there’s a margin associated with that.

In some cases with the larger companies it’s a smaller margin because of their buying power, but they’re usually buying in components, assembling them, often using a technology that’s less effective than ours is.

Once you stack those margins, you have a very low margin to deal with on an operating level.

That’s why almost 100% of the module makers in the world are located in China.
Exactly. Where the manufacturing capacity is.

Chapter 8: Why semiconductor-style assembly changes gross margins

7:02
Exactly.

However, our whole thesis is to semiconductorize the assembly of these devices.

So we build them 500 at a time, not one at a time.

We use semiconductor capital equipment to do everything from the wafers through to the assembly through to the test.

So we have an inherent advantage built in both labor and capital that should make our gross margins look more like semiconductor gross margins than like our competitors.

Okay, I wanted to ask you about the competitive landscape here.

We had quite a few questions from the community. I’ll try to pare them down to just a few.

The 800G and 1.6T transceiver market is getting crowded.

Giants like Marvell are moving very fast in this space.

The company says the optical interposer is a gamechanger for both heat and power consumption.

We haven’t seen a tier-one CSP attached to your technology yet.

Who is the big fish? Is there a big fish that validates this technology for investors?

Chapter 9: Competitive landscape — Marvell, Celestial AI & NDAs

8:19
There are, and there are the hyperscalers.
We’re addressing those hyperscalers.

What we have to demonstrate in order to sell our products to hyperscalers, even if we’re selling as a component supplier to another company, is that we can actually manufacture.

So we’re very focused today on our manufacturing capability and taking what we have existing as a 1 million unit capacity to assemble our devices to 5 and 10 million units.

It’s not until that time that you can demonstrate that a hyperscale customer is really going to interact with you because they need to be confident that once they commit to your product, you’re going to be able to supply them.

Are you in an NDA with a hyperscaler right now?

9:21
We’re in NDAs with suppliers to hyperscalers.

Okay.

And what do you make of the Marvell comparison? Where does POET sit in the stack? Are you competing with them? Are you enabling them?

Chapter 10: POET’s role — competing with or enabling Marvell?

9:36
We’re not trying to build GPU devices, which I think is what their most recent announcement was about.

We’re a supplier to Marvell now that they’ve acquired Celestial AI, who has been a customer of ours for a couple of years.

What we supply to Celestial AI are light sources — high-bandwidth, multi-frequency, high-power light sources that light up the photonic fabric that Celestial AI talks about as being the communication device between GPUs and one GPU and another GPU, or a GPU and a memory device.

So we think that the light source market, and what we have to offer in that light source market, isn’t fully reflected in our market cap.

We showed what we call the Blazar back at OFC in San Francisco in 2025. Nobody got it.
This time around in Los Angeles, we were visited by several laser companies who now understand that they need an approach to laser development that’s very similar to what ours is.

Chapter 11: The Blazar light source — OFC reception & laser company interest

11:09
All right. You perhaps won’t be surprised, but the investors in our community are in the weeds on some of the technical details.

I’ll close with a couple of their questions.

We’ll do a little bit of a lightning round here.

With Marvell’s acquisition of Celestial AI, which you just mentioned, and the NVLink Fusion partnership reshaping the CPO supply chain, what’s the clearest near-term milestone investors should watch as evidence that POET’s optical interposer is moving from design wins into recognized revenue? What’s a realistic time frame?

Chapter 12: Milestone investors should watch for recognized revenue
11:55
We have an invoice from Celestial AI, which we’re intending to ship product against, and that’ll be shipped this year.

In fact, I believe some of it will be shipped next quarter.

That light source product is the product that we’re going to be selling to Marvell and to Celestial AI.

I think that will assist them in what they’re doing internally to develop those GPUs.

Okay, last couple here from StockTwits MMC.
Is there anything you can offer on Foxconn and Luxshare — validation is done, modules have been produced — and have you explored what can be announced regarding Marvell that’s acceptable to both legal teams?

Chapter 13: Foxconn, Luxshare & Marvell purchase order update

12:45
I’m not able to say much about Foxconn or Luxshare.

That’s one of the challenges we’ve had, which is when you’re dealing with large companies who have committed to designing modules using your components, sometimes you hear from them and sometimes you don’t.

We expect to hear from at least one of those.

With Marvell, it’s a different story.

We’ve got a purchase order from them that we’re going to ship against.

And we intend to continue that relationship and build it over time.
And last one here.

Without naming customers, what does a meaningful order look like for POET in volume or revenue, and how quickly can it scale once it starts?

Chapter 14: What does a meaningful order look like for POET?

13:38
We think the $5 million purchase order that we got for our 800G optical engines is a meaningful order because it helps us with bedding down our manufacturing effort.

But we’re certainly expecting this year purchase orders that are far in excess of that $5 million.

And I don’t think people will be disappointed."}}}, "rest_id": "2046621787203784936", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "14380", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2046665382170386532", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/iphone" rel="nofollow">Twitter for iPhone</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "5140", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Meet the people leading the IEEE Photonics Society! In our latest IEEE Photonics Newsroom leadership spotlight, we feature Astghik Chalyan, AVP of STEM Outreach, STEM Outreach Officer at VUB B-PHOT Brussels Photonics, and CEO of EYEST. Her journey from hands-on student experiments to global impact is nothing short of inspiring!

From empowering teachers through the Photonics Explorer Kit to leading international outreach initiatives, Astghik shares how early exposure, mentorship, and real-world connections can spark lifelong curiosity in STEM and photonics.

💡 Why does outreach matter?
💡 How can scientists better connect with the public?
💡 And what role do educators play in building the next generation of innovators?

Discover her story, insights, and practical advice for anyone passionate about making a difference. Read the full interview and get inspired: https://t.co/NEC2qd1koR

#Photonics #STEMed #IEEE #STEMOutreach #WomenInSTEM #Leadership

text:
The idea U.S. retail investors can save European photonics shops from hostile actors is evocative, and probably true

Either way, it's the most coherent geopolitical strategy I've heard in a while https://t.co/qU2ltGGyvC
